questions.question_text: A married couple in Minnesota owns a home titled solely in the husband's name. The husband wants to sell. Who must sign the deed?
questions.correct_answer: Both spouses must sign the deed
distractors: Only the husband since he holds title
Only the spouse with higher income
Neither spouse if a power of attorney exists
questions.explanation: Minnesota homestead law requires both spouses to sign the deed for homestead property, even when title is held in only one spouse's name. This protects the non-titled spouse's homestead interest in the property.

questions.question_text: Minnesota follows which system of marital property ownership?
distractors: Community property with equal division
Tenancy by the entirety exclusively
Joint ownership mandated by statute
questions.explanation: Minnesota is a common law (separate property) state that uses equitable distribution upon divorce. It is not a community property state. Courts divide marital property equitably, which may not mean equally, based on various statutory factors.

questions.question_text: A wife refuses to sign the deed for the family homestead her husband wants to sell. If the husband signs and delivers a deed to a buyer anyway, the conveyance is:
questions.correct_answer: Void, even though the husband holds sole title
distractors: Valid, because only the titleholder must sign
Voidable at the buyer's option for one year
Valid if the buyer did not know the seller was married
questions.explanation: Under Minnesota Statutes section 507.02, a married owner cannot convey the homestead without the signatures of both spouses, with narrow exceptions such as a purchase-money mortgage or a conveyance between spouses. The Minnesota Supreme Court has held that a homestead conveyance signed by only one spouse is not merely voidable but void — the buyer acquires no interest. There is no court-order procedure to override a competent spouse's refusal to sign; only the spouse (or the spouse's own attorney-in-fact) can supply the required signature.

questions.question_text: A single person who solely owns their home in Minnesota wants to sell. Regarding spousal consent, the seller must:
questions.correct_answer: Sign the deed without spousal consent needed
distractors: Obtain an affidavit of single status first
Have two witnesses verify unmarried status
File a homestead waiver with the county
questions.explanation: The spousal consent requirement for homestead conveyance applies only to married individuals. A single homeowner can convey their property by signing the deed alone. While title companies may request documentation of single status, it is not a statutory requirement for the conveyance itself.

questions.question_text: During a divorce, the court divides the couple's real property. Minnesota's equitable distribution approach means the court will:
questions.correct_answer: Divide property fairly based on statutory factors
distractors: Split all assets exactly 50/50 automatically
Award property to whoever holds the title
Give all real estate to the higher-earning spouse
questions.explanation: Equitable distribution in Minnesota means the court divides property fairly, considering factors like each spouse's contributions, length of marriage, and economic circumstances. It does not mandate an equal 50/50 split or automatic title-based allocation.

questions.question_text: A developer plans to sell lots in a new Minnesota subdivision. Before marketing, the developer must register with:
distractors: The county recorder's office only
The local planning and zoning board
The Minnesota Attorney General's office
questions.explanation: Under the Minnesota Subdivided Land Sales Practices Act (Minn. Stat. 83.20-83.42), it is unlawful to offer or sell interests in subdivided lands unless they are registered with the commissioner of commerce, so the Department of Commerce is the registering agency. Note that exemptions exist — for example, offerings of ten or fewer lots and land inside a municipality that has adopted subdivision regulations. County recording and local zoning approvals may also be needed, but they are not the registration this act requires.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases a lot in a registered Minnesota subdivision. Before or at the time of sale, the buyer must receive:
distractors: A certified environmental impact study
An appraisal from a state-approved appraiser
A title opinion from the county attorney
questions.explanation: The Subdivided Lands Act requires developers to provide buyers with a public offering statement (property report) that discloses material information about the subdivision. Environmental studies, appraisals, and county attorney opinions are not the specific documents required under this act.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a contract to purchase a subdivision lot and receives the property report the next day. The buyer decides to cancel. Under the Subdivided Lands Act, the buyer:
questions.correct_answer: Has a right of rescission after receiving it
distractors: Cannot cancel once the contract is signed
Must pay a 10% cancellation penalty
Can only cancel within 24 hours of signing
questions.explanation: Under Minn. Stat. 83.28, the buyer has an unconditional right to rescind at any time before, or within five days after, actually receiving both the binding contract and the public offering statement — so the clock here starts when the report is received. If the statement is never provided as required, the contract is voidable by the buyer for up to three years. There is no cancellation penalty, and the rescission right cannot be waived.

questions.question_text: A developer sells subdivision lots without registering with the Department of Commerce. A buyer discovers this after closing. The buyer's strongest legal claim is:
questions.correct_answer: Violation of the Subdivided Lands Act
distractors: Breach of the implied warranty of fitness
Violation of the federal RESPA statute
A claim under adverse possession doctrine
questions.explanation: Selling lots without proper registration under Minnesota's Subdivided Lands Act (Ch. 83.20-83.45) is a direct violation of state law. This gives the buyer a strong legal claim. RESPA, implied warranties, and adverse possession address different legal issues.

questions.question_text: A developer markets Minnesota subdivision lots online to out-of-state buyers without registering with the Department of Commerce. A buyer from Iowa purchases a lot and later wants to rescind. What is the likely outcome?
questions.correct_answer: The buyer can rescind because the subdivision was unregistered
distractors: The buyer cannot rescind because they are out of state
The sale stands because online marketing is exempt
Only Minnesota residents are protected by the act
questions.explanation: The Subdivided Lands Act requires registration before selling lots regardless of the buyer's location or marketing method. An unregistered sale gives the buyer grounds for rescission. The act protects all purchasers, not just Minnesota residents.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller verbally agree to the sale of a residential property in Minnesota. This agreement is:
questions.correct_answer: Unenforceable under the statute of frauds
distractors: Valid if both parties have witnesses present
Enforceable once the buyer pays earnest money
Binding if a handshake occurs before a notary
questions.explanation: Minnesota's statute of frauds requires contracts for the sale of real property to be in writing. Verbal agreements for real estate sales are unenforceable regardless of witnesses, earnest money payments, or notarized handshakes.

questions.question_text: Under Minnesota law, which of the following agreements must be in writing?
distractors: An agreement to schedule a home inspection
A verbal offer to purchase personal property
A month-to-month residential lease
questions.explanation: Minnesota's real estate license law (Minn. Stat. 82.66) requires both listing agreements and buyer representation (buyer's broker) agreements to be in writing and signed, and the statute of frauds (Minn. Stat. 513.04-513.05) requires contracts for the sale of land and leases longer than one year to be written. Scheduling a home inspection, an offer for personal property, and a month-to-month residential lease need not be in writing.

questions.question_text: A listing agreement in Minnesota must include all of the following EXCEPT:
distractors: Identification of the parties involved
A description of the property being sold
The terms and conditions of the agreement
questions.explanation: Minn. Stat. 82.66, subd. 1 requires listing agreements to be in writing and to include items such as a definite expiration date, a description of the property, the list price and seller's terms, the compensation or how it is computed, and the events that entitle the broker to a commission. The seller's original purchase price is not a required element.

questions.question_text: A buyer signs a purchase agreement electronically using a qualified digital platform. Under Minnesota's adoption of UETA, this signature is:
distractors: Invalid because real estate requires ink signatures
Only valid for contracts under $50,000
Enforceable only if notarized within 30 days
questions.explanation: Minnesota has adopted the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), which recognizes electronic signatures as valid and legally binding for real estate contracts. There is no requirement for ink signatures, dollar thresholds, or subsequent notarization.

questions.question_text: A buyer verbally agrees to purchase land, takes possession, and makes substantial improvements. The seller later claims the contract is unenforceable. The buyer may argue:
questions.correct_answer: Partial performance may overcome the writing rule
distractors: Verbal land contracts are always enforceable
Improvements automatically transfer title to buyer
The statute of frauds never applies to land
questions.explanation: Minnesota recognizes a limited partial performance exception to the statute of frauds. When a buyer has taken possession and made substantial improvements in reliance on a verbal agreement, courts may enforce the contract despite the lack of a writing. However, this exception is narrowly applied.
